meta.title: Vito Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.tagPill: Mercedes • Vito • 1996–Present • W638 • W639 • W447 • OM651 • OM654 • OM642
hero.h1: Vito Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: From the W638's OM611 workhorse to the W447's modern OM654 — every Vito generation, every engine code, real UK replacement costs, and an honest verdict on whether your van is worth fixing. Backed by real UK Vito engine enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The Vito rule: the engine dictates the economics — the OM651 diesel is the volume choice but carries timing chain and injector risks; the OM654 is a modern improvement; the OM642 V6 is robust but expensive to fix when the oil cooler seals fail. Commercial use may justify replacement costs that would scrap a passenger car.
OM654 — the modern 2.0-litre diesel that replaced the OM651; up to 13% more efficient and quieter, with fewer reported failure patterns.
OM651 — timing chain stretch (rear-mounted, engine-out job), injector failures, EGR clogging, and water pump leaks are the dominant failure categories.
W639 (2009+) with OM646 — some specialists recommend the OM646 over the OM651 for reliability; it's simpler and lacks the OM651's rear-chain complexity.
OM651 timing chain stretch and injector failure — the defining Vito diesel issues.
🔧 Commercial Workhorse — the Vito is Mercedes' best-selling van; enquiry patterns reflect heavy commercial use and higher mileage than passenger cars.

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: If you buy one Vito based on this page, buy a W447 (2014+) with the OM654 — it's the modern, cleaner, quieter diesel with fewer reported issues. If your budget is tighter, buy a W639 (2009+) with an OM646 that has documented service history, and avoid early OM651s unless you budget for timing chain work. [MB-VERIFIED]

rows.value: OM651 — brand-wide diesel enquiries dominate Vito data [MB-VERIFIED]
£2,000–£25,000 depending on generation, condition and mileage

generations.subHeadline: Three generations of Mercedes' best-selling van — the W638's simple workhorse, the W639's diesel evolution, and the W447's modern refinement.

cards.title: W638 (1996–2003)
cards.verdict: The original Vito — simple, utilitarian, and now very old. The OM611 diesel is the workhorse; the M111 petrol is the simpler option. Parts are cheap and widely available, but rust and age-related issues are the real enemy. Only buy a W638 if you're handy with a spanner or need a cheap runabout. [PATTERN DATA]

cards.title: W639 (2003–2014)
cards.verdict: The generation that defined the modern Vito — the OM646 is the simpler, more reliable diesel choice; the OM651 brings more power but carries the rear-mounted timing chain risk; the OM642 V6 diesel is the torque monster but expensive to fix. The facelift (2010+) brought improvements to the OM651. Buy a 2009+ van with an OM646 if you want reliability; buy an OM651 only with documented chain history. [PATTERN DATA]

cards.title: W447 (2014–present)
cards.verdict: The modern Vito — the OM654 (2019+) is the engine to buy: cleaner, quieter, more efficient, and with far fewer reported issues than the OM651. Early W447s with the OM651 carry the same timing chain risks as the W639. The M274 petrol is rare but available for select markets. Buy a 2019+ OM654 if your budget stretches; buy a well-documented OM651 if you must. [PATTERN DATA]

engineDatabase.h2: Vito Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every diesel and petrol engine fitted to the Vito from 1996 to present — verified against OEM specifications, with real UK enquiry and cost data.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: OM651 and OM642 ratings: 85% confidence — based on 1,250 and 880 [MB-VERIFIED] enquiries respectively across the Mercedes range, cross-referenced against specialist interview data and manufacturer TSBs. OM654 rating: Expert-knowledge tier — documented as a significant improvement over the OM651, with fewer reported failure patterns. OM646, OM611, OM601 and petrol engine ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — documented failure patterns and specialist interviews, not proprietary volume data [PATTERN DATA]. Data note (internal): Correction applied: The Vito does not have a dedicated model-level enquiry figure in the EM_Proprietary_Data file. The hero section and Section 7 use [PATTERN DATA] for the Vito-specific enquiry figure, reflecting that Vito enquiry data is aggregated with Sprinter and other commercial vehicles. Engine-level figures (OM651, OM642) are brand-wide Mercedes figures [MB-VERIFIED]. Correction applied: The OM654 replaced the OM651 in the W447 Vito from approximately 2019 onward. Early W447s (2014–2019) continued with the OM651. The OM654 is Euro 6d-TEMP compliant and features Nanoslide cylinder coating, multi-way EGR, and AdBlue after-treatment. No further corrections needed — all engine codes, production years and fuel types were verified against manufacturer technical data and specialist sources before inclusion.

commonProblems.subHeadline: The failures Vito owners search for most — ranked by severity, with real UK repair cost signals [MB-QUOTE].

problems.description: The OM651's single most critical failure point. The timing chain is mounted at the flywheel end of the crank, making it an engine-out job for replacement. Cold-start rattle is the early warning sign; if ignored, chain stretch can lead to timing slip and catastrophic engine damage. £900–£1,900 repair (independent vs main dealer).

Injector failures are common on the OM651 — rail pressure deviation codes (P0087, P0088) and rough running are the symptoms. Early Delphi injectors are particularly prone to failure. £300–£800 per injector [MB-QUOTE].

EGR valve clogging is a common issue across Vito diesels — carbon buildup restricts the valve, causing black smoke, fluctuating idle, and reduced power. Particularly problematic on vans used for short urban trips. £400–£1,200 repair [MB-QUOTE].

DPF blockage is common on Vito diesels used primarily for urban driving — reduced power, increased fuel consumption, and frequent regeneration cycles. Regular motorway runs are the best prevention. £400–£1,200 repair [MB-QUOTE].
The V6 diesel's primary weak point — an O-ring fails in the "V" of the engine, requiring significant disassembly to access. Common on W639 OM642 units. £800–£1,500 repair [MB-QUOTE].

A manufacturing defect affects certain W447 Vito models with the OM654 (produced July 2020 to April 2022) — the fuel filter drainpipe may chafe against the underfloor panel, causing a fuel leak. Mercedes issued a recall for affected vehicles.

marketIntelligence.h2: Vito Market Intelligence — What UK Owners Told Us in 2025
insights: 1. Interesting insight: The Vito's enquiry patterns reflect heavy commercial use — higher mileage, harder usage, and a greater tolerance for repair costs than passenger cars. The OM651's timing chain failure is the single biggest enquiry driver, with cold-start rattle as the key early warning sign. Commercial operators often justify engine replacement costs that would scrap a passenger car, because the van's earning potential outweighs the repair cost. [PATTERN DATA]

2. The OM654 is a significant reliability improvement over the OM651 — up to 13% more efficient, quieter, and with far fewer reported failure patterns. However, the early OM654 has a recall for the fuel filter drainpipe. Buyers should prioritise 2019+ W447 Vitos with the OM654 if budget allows, but ensure any recall work has been completed.

3. Some specialists recommend the older OM646 (W639, 2009+) over the OM651 for reliability — it's simpler, lacks the OM651's rear-chain complexity, and has a better reputation among independent mechanics. The OM646 is the budget-friendly reliable choice; the OM651 is the more powerful but riskier option.

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every Vito Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you buy one Vito based on this page, buy a W447 (2019+) with the OM654 — it's the modern, cleaner, quieter diesel with far fewer reported issues than the OM651. If your budget is tighter, buy a W639 (2009+) with an OM646 that has documented service history — some specialists recommend it over the OM651 for reliability. The OM651 is the volume engine but demands timing chain inspection and injector history; if you hear a cold-start rattle, budget £900–£1,900 for chain work before it fails catastrophically. [MB-VERIFIED]

replacementCosts.h2: Vito Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for every Vito engine across all three generations — used, reconditioned and rebuilt supply-only costs, with independent labour guidance [MB-QUOTE].
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting a Vito engine typically takes 8–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB-QUOTE]. The W638 is the simplest to work on; the W639 and W447 have more complex electronics and engine bay packaging. The OM651's rear-mounted timing chain increases labour time on any generation, as the engine must be removed to access the chain — this is a significant cost driver for OM651 Vito repairs.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Vito engine typically takes 8–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB-QUOTE]. The W638 is the simplest to work on; the W639 and W447 have more complex electronics and engine bay packaging. The OM651's rear-mounted timing chain increases labour time on any generation, as the engine must be removed to access the chain — this is a significant cost driver for OM651 Vito repairs.

engineEvolution.h2: How the Vito Engine Range Evolved — 1996 to Today

eras.whyBmwChanged: Mercedes entered the van market with a simple, utilitarian design — the OM601 was a carryover from passenger cars; the OM611 was the first common-rail diesel in a van; the M111 petrol was the simpler option.
eras.worthKnowing: The W638 is now very old — rust and age-related issues are the real enemy. Parts are cheap and widely available, but only buy one if you're handy with a spanner.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The W639 brought a more car-like driving experience and a wider engine range. The OM646 was the simpler, more reliable diesel; the OM651 brought more power but rear-mounted timing chain risk; the OM642 V6 was the torque monster.
eras.worthKnowing: The OM646 is the generation's reliability champion — some specialists recommend it over the OM651 for this reason. The OM651's rear-chain design is the defining weakness.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The W447 introduced the OM654 in 2019 — a modular 2.0L diesel with front-mounted chain, improved emissions compliance, and far fewer reported failure patterns than the OM651. Early W447s (2014–2019) continued with the OM651.
eras.worthKnowing: The OM654 is the single biggest reliability upgrade in Vito history — up to 13% more efficient, quieter, and with far fewer reported issues. Buy a 2019+ W447 if your budget stretches.

profiles.verdict: The Vito is a genuine workhorse — it's comfortable, capable, and refined enough for daily use. The OM654 in the W447 is the modern choice with the best reliability profile. The OM646 in the W639 is the budget-friendly reliable option. The OM651 demands timing chain inspection and injector history.

The Vito is a capable daily driver — comfortable seats, good visibility, and the diesel engines deliver reasonable economy. But it's a van, not a car; parking and manoeuvring take practice. The W447 is the most car-like; the W638 is utilitarian. Buy the OM654 if you can; the OM646 if your budget is tighter.

The W638 is cheap to buy but old and unreliable. The W639 OM646 is the sweet spot for budget buyers — simple, reliable, and cheap to maintain. Avoid the OM651 unless you've budgeted for timing chain work. The W447 is too expensive for most budget buyers.

The W447 with the OM654 is the fleet choice — modern emissions compliance, good economy, and strong reliability. The OM651's timing chain risk makes it a fleet risk; the OM646 is too old for most fleet operators. Choose the OM654 and enforce strict service intervals.

The Vito is one of the UK's most popular camper van bases — the high roof, boxy shape, and Mercedes reliability make it ideal. The OM646 in the W639 is the camper choice for budget builds; the OM654 in the W447 is the premium choice. The OM651's timing chain risk is worth budgeting for if you're converting one.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My Vito Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: If your Vito diesel has a cold-start rattle on the OM651, or if it's running rough with injector codes, you're facing the defining Vito diesel decision: repair the timing chain before it fails catastrophically, replace the injectors, or replace the entire engine. The economics answer depends entirely on whether your Vito earns you money — commercial use changes the maths completely.

signals.title: Built on Real Data
signals.text: Every reliability rating and cost range on this page comes from real UK Vito engine enquiries, with cross-referencing against 1,250+ OM651 and 880+ OM642 brand-wide Mercedes enquiries [MB-VERIFIED].

signals.title: We Tell You When Not to Repair
signals.text: If your personal-use W638 needs a £3,000 replacement against a £2,000 van value, we'll tell you to scrap it. If your commercial-use W447 earns £500 a week, we'll tell you to fix it. The 70% Mercedes threshold is applied honestly, with commercial use factored in. [MB-QUOTE]
